content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
July 8,
1993
Cheryl L. Pontious
709 Tamarack Drive
Union City,
CA
94587
Re:
Your ReqUest for Advice
Our File No. A-93-260
Dear Ms. Pontious:
I am writing to confirm our telephone conversation on
June 30,
1993 wherein we discussed your request for written
advice.
You are concerned about potential conflict—of—interest
situations because you intend to be a candidate for city council
in Union City and your husband is employed by the Union City
Police Department.
You inquired if you would have a conflict of
interest under the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”)
1
,
due to your
husband’s position in the city police department.
Under the Act,
your husband’s position does not prohibit you
from running for city council.
However, the Act does prohibit a
public official from making, participating in making, br otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Section
87103 provides in pertinent part:
An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
or on:
*
*
*
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000
g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—93—260
Page
2
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
Your spouse is a salaried employee of the Union City Police
Department.
Section 82030(a), which defines “income,” provides
that the income of an individual also includes any community
property interest in the income of a spouse.
However, salary and
reimbursement for expenses or per diem received from a state,
local or federal government agency is expressly exempted from the
definition of “income” for purposes of the Act.
(Section
82030(b) (2).)
Consequently, the salary your husband receives from
the city police department will not create a conflict of interest
for you with respect to decisions affecting the police department.
The Act also provides that a conflict of interest may exist
where a decision will result in a public official’s personal
expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities or those of his or her
immediate family increasing or decreasing by $250, irrespective of
the source of the increase or decrease.
(Regulation
18702.1(a) (4),
copy enclosed.)
This provision contemplates
situations where increases and decreases to income received by a
public ‘official or his or her spouse from a local governmental
agency can be potentially disqualifying.
However, notwithstanding Regulation 18702.1(a) (4)
above,
an
official does not have to disqualify from participating in a
governmental decision if the decision only affects the salary, per
diem or reimbursement for expenses the official or his or her
spouse receives from a state or local government agency.
(Regulation 18702.1(c) (1).)
Thus, if a decision comes before the
city council that will affect the salary of your spouse,
you would
not be required to disqualify from the decision.
However, please note that the exception in Regulation
18702.1(c)
does not apply to decisions to hire, fire, promote,
demote or discipline your spouse, or to set a salary for your
spouse which is different from salaries paid to other employees of
the district in the same job classification or position.
(Regulation 18702.l(c)(1).)
In that situation,
you will be
required to disqualify yourself.
In summary, your husband’s position with the police
department willnot result in a conflict of interest for you
unless a city council decision will result in the hiring, firing,
promotion,
demotion or disciplining of your spouse, or the
establishment of a salary for your spouse which is different from
salaries paid to other employees of the police department in the
same job classification or position.


File No.
A—93—260
Page
3
I trust this answers your question.
Should further questions
arise, please contact our office at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
JM:JS:ak
Jeff Marschner
General Counsel
By:
Legal Division
Enclosure

sections.question: You inquired if you would have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act (the "Act"), due to your husband's position in the city police department.
sections.conclusion: In summary, your husband's position with the police department will not result in a conflict of interest for you unless a city council decision will result in the hiring, firing, promotion, demotion or disciplining of your spouse, or the establishment of a salary for your spouse which is different from salaries paid to other employees of the police department in the same job classification or position.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a candidate for city council have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act if their spouse is employed by the city police department?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A spouse's employment with the city police department does not create a conflict of interest for a city council candidate, except when decisions involve hiring, firing, promotion, demotion, discipline, or setting a different salary for the spouse compared to other employees in the same position.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard FPPC advice letter format with clear question and conclusion sections. Document contains detailed regulatory citations and statutory references typical of FPPC guidance.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You inquired if you would have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act (the "Act"), due to your husband's position in the city police department.

CONCLUSION: In summary, your husband's position with the police department will not result in a conflict of interest for you unless a city council decision will result in the hiring, firing, promotion, demotion or disciplining of your spouse, or the establishment of a salary for your spouse which is different from salaries paid to other employees of the police department in the same job classification or position.

You are concerned about potential conflict—of—interest situations because you intend to be a candidate for city council in Union City and your husband is employed by the Union City Police Department. You inquired if you would have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) 1 , due to your husband’s position in the city police department. Under the Act, your husband’s position does not prohibit you from running for city council. However, the Act does prohibit a public official from making, participating in making, br otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides in pertinent part: An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on: * * * (c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. Your spouse is a salaried employee of the Union City Police Department. Section 82030(a), which defines “income,” provides that the income of an individual also includes any community property interest in the income of a spouse. However, salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem received from a state, local or federal government agency is expressly exempted from the definition of “income” for purposes of the Act. embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to Cheryl L. Pontious regarding potential conflicts of interest for her candidacy for Union City city council given her husband's employment with the Union City Police Department. The FPPC concludes that her husband's salary and routine personnel matters do not create conflicts, but she must disqualify herself from decisions involving hiring, firing, promotion, demotion, discipline, or setting a different salary for her spouse.
